title: You Can't Replace the Spirit with a Substance
speaker: Pastor Tito Lyro
source_files: You Can't Replace the Spirit with a Substance - Pastor Tito Lyro - Ephesians 5_18-21.txt
outline: Introduction: Ephesians 5:18-21 — be filled with the Spirit
The contrast: do not be drunk with wine vs. be filled with the Spirit
Why substances cannot replace the Spirit
The marks of Spirit-filling: psalms, hymns, spiritual songs
Always giving thanks for all things in the name of Jesus
Submitting to one another in the fear of God
Conclusion: The Spirit-filled life as the only adequate substitute
summary: Pastor Tito Lyro preaches from Ephesians 5:18-21, beginning another pass at this passage (noting they will return to Ephesians 5 a few more times). He focuses on Paul's command to be filled with the Spirit rather than drunk with wine, showing that no substance can replace the Spirit's work — which produces worship, thanksgiving, and mutual submission.
transcript:
We're going to turn again to Ephesians chapter 5. We're likely going to turn to Ephesians 5 a few more times before we're done with this chapter.

> *"See then that you walk circumspectly, not as fools but as wise, redeeming the time, because the days are evil. Therefore do not be unwise, but understand what the will of the Lord is. And do not be drunk with wine, in which is dissipation; but be filled with the Spirit, speaking to one another in psalms and hymns and spiritual songs, singing and making melody in your heart to the Lord, giving thanks always for all things to God the Father in the name of our Lord Jesus Christ, submitting to one another in the fear of God."* (Ephesians 5:15–21)

## Walking in Wisdom

In this portion of Ephesians 5, Paul is telling us that a person who has been redeemed by the Lord Jesus Christ walks — or lives — wisely. We've already seen that the Christian walks in love and as a child of light. Now Paul finishes with a third walk: the Christian lives carefully, thoughtfully, in wisdom.

Walking in wisdom means living in practice according to what we know the Bible teaches. It's not complicated. The Bible says something; we live according to that. That's what wisdom is.

Paul gives a non-exhaustive list of what it means to walk in wisdom here in chapter 5:15–21. I do not believe there is any exhaustive list in the New Testament of either sins or righteous behaviors. These are like case laws, given so that we can understand how God operates, what he expects, and then live out of those principles in daily life.

Here Paul tells us that walking in wisdom means:
1. Taking the opportunities the Lord gives us to share the gospel with others (verse 16, "redeeming the time")
2. Understanding the will of the Lord and living according to it
3. Not replacing the Holy Spirit with a substance (verse 18a)
4. Being filled with the Holy Spirit, which produces speaking to one another in psalms and hymns and spiritual songs, singing and making melody in your heart, giving thanks always for all things, and submitting to one another in the fear of Christ

Notice the major pattern in this list: wise living is others-oriented for the sake of Christ. The things Paul says we're supposed to do always focus on other people and on Christ. A wise life is a life lived toward other people.

## Why Drunkenness Here?

This morning we'll consider the fact — and I mean that as a fact, not as an interpretation — that you cannot walk in wisdom by replacing the Holy Spirit with a substance. That substance could be legal, like alcohol or marijuana, or illicit, like cocaine, heroin, or mushrooms. None of those things can replace what only the Spirit can do.

We're going to focus on the first half of verse 18, where Paul says, *"Do not be drunk with wine, in which is dissipation."* This is not going to be a sermon about not drinking, because if I told you not to drink I'd be going against the Scriptures. But it is a sermon about reliance on the Spirit instead of substances.

A question that came to me as I was studying this passage: why is drunkenness here? Why does Paul use this particular sin to contrast with being filled with the Spirit? Commentary literature on this passage generally just passes over that question without trying to answer it. After some reading, I found a suggestion that makes sense. Let me give you two reasons why Paul includes drunkenness here as the contrast to being filled with the Spirit.

First, substance abuse — and that's what drunkenness is — has been and continues to be a serious problem in the world and in the church. Right after the flood, the very first sin recorded of Noah is that he became drunk. The Corinthian church struggled with this at the Lord's Supper, where some people would drink all the wine and get drunk while others got nothing.

Second, and more pertinent: it was common among pagan religions to try to achieve closeness with their gods by getting high. Achieving an ecstasy through being drunk on alcohol or through mind-altering drugs was seen as the ultimate way to commune with the gods. You may have heard of the Delphic Oracle in ancient Greece — a group of priestesses who would consume some kind of mind-altering substance, speak in gibberish, and have interpreters declare what the gods were saying about the future. The way Paul writes this command — *do not be drunk* — infers with a high degree of certainty that some of the Ephesian Christians were trying to continue that practice, believing that getting drunk or high would bring them closer to God.

And I'll be obvious: when it says *don't be drunk with wine*, wine represents all alcoholic beverages, and I believe also all mind-altering drugs. Paul is not saying that vodka is okay. The emphasis is on the drunkenness, not on the particular means of getting there.

## What Is Drunkenness?

Paul says drunkenness is the opposite of being filled with the Spirit, because you cannot replace the Holy Spirit with a substance. Getting drunk will not bring you closer to God or solve anything in the long run. Getting drunk or high may help you escape reality for a moment, and you may even feel in your stupor that you are closer to God — but when you sober up, reality is still the same, and there is likely more destruction around you.

Now, it's difficult to define drunkenness precisely because alcohol works differently in different people. But at the very least, Scripture equates being drunk with an altered state of mind caused by alcohol or other substances. Proverbs 23:29-35 gives us the picture:

> *"Who has woe? Who has sorrow? Who has contentions? Who has complaints? Who has wounds without cause? Who has redness of eyes? Those who linger long at the wine, those who go in search of mixed wine. Do not look on the wine when it is red, when it sparkles in the cup, when it swirls around smoothly; at the last it bites like a serpent, and stings like a viper... Yes, you will be like one who lies down in the midst of the sea, or like one who lies at the top of the mast... 'They have struck me, but I was not hurt; they have beaten me, but I did not feel it. When shall I awake, that I may seek another drink?'"*

That last line is devastating: after everything is destroyed, the only thought the drunkard can have is when can I get drunk again. Nothing else matters.

It's important to realize there's more to drunkenness than just the chemical effects of alcohol on your brain or how many drinks you've had. There's a relationship with alcohol that is idolatrous — you're trying to get from the substance something that only God can give you, and you're willing to destroy everything in your life for that. That is nothing more than religious worship. The most secular drunkard is worshiping something.

The drunkard is looking for, in drunkenness, what only the Spirit of God can provide: peace instead of anxiety, escape from problems, sleep. People may feel better and closer to God in their drunkenness or under the influence of mind-altering drugs — but that is a misguided feeling.

## Drunkenness Is Dissipation

Paul describes being drunk as *dissipation* — which means behavior that shows a lack of concern or thought for the consequences of one's actions. It also means a type of behavior that cannot be saved or redeemed. The word dissipation itself is literally a negation of the word for salvation. That means you cannot drink or get high for the glory of God. It is an impossibility. That behavior cannot be redeemed, cannot be done for the glory of God.

This is the word used to describe the way of life of the prodigal son. Luke 15:13 says the younger son *"squandered his property in reckless living"* — that word *reckless living* is the same Greek word: dissipation. This is the kind of behavior that doesn't take into consideration anyone else but the person doing it.

So if you are a drunkard, you are not a victim. You're not the one let down by society. You are a selfish person who is destroying everything around you in an activity that cannot be redeemed or done for the glory of God. It absolutely does not think of the well-being of anyone else — and it is the opposite of being filled with the Spirit, which is demonstrated by an others-orientation in all we do.

Because drunkenness is inherently selfish, it will destroy relationships all around it. It is virtually universal that the drunkard will have destroyed or will destroy family and friends and every relationship around them. And it's interesting that drunkenness is the one major social sin that even our culture still considers taboo — because of the obvious effect it has on society and families. And yet we're no longer willing to call it sin. Drunkenness is no longer sin in the eyes of the world; it's supposed to be a disease.

## A Journey Through the Bible on Drunkenness

Let me take you through what the Bible says about drunkenness — and I'll tell you, it's never spoken of as a disease or a good thing. It's always spoken of as sin. The only book of the Bible that speaks of drunkenness positively is Song of Solomon, where being drunk with the love of your spouse is used as a metaphor — which is clearly different from what we're talking about.

In Deuteronomy 21, the incorrigible son who dishonors his parents is described as a drunkard. In Deuteronomy 29, if you are cursed under the covenant you're described as a drunkard. Proverbs says the drunkard will come to poverty. In Isaiah, Jeremiah, Ezekiel, and all the Minor Prophets, drunkenness is time and again used as a symbol of being cursed. According to Jesus in Luke 21:34, drunkenness is the result of not focusing on Christ. Romans 13:13-14 tells us that drunkenness is a work of the flesh. In 1 Thessalonians 5:7-8, drunkenness is a characteristic of those who are not watching and therefore not hoping for the return of Christ.

And critically — because we don't often think about it this way — the drunkard will not inherit the kingdom of God. 1 Corinthians 6:10 is clear:

> *"...nor thieves, nor the covetous, nor drunkards, nor revilers, nor extortioners will inherit the kingdom of God."*

This is a problem with the idea that drunkenness is a disease, and with the AA teaching that once a drunkard, always a drunkard. A Christian may struggle with drunkenness, but that is not his or her identity. Either you're a drunkard, or you're a Christian. To say that a drunkard is always going to be a drunkard for life no matter what is to say that a person addicted to pornography is a pornography addict for life, or that a child abuser is an abuser for life and we should just accept it.

But if you're in Christ, you're a Christian — not anything else. You've been redeemed. Read the rest of 1 Corinthians 6:11:

> *"And such were some of you. But you were washed, but you were sanctified, but you were justified in the name of the Lord Jesus and by the Spirit of our God."*

That's true of anyone who has come to Christ. You were a sinner, but you came to Christ. You have been washed by his blood. Your sin does not characterize you — Christ does. That's how sin no longer has dominion over us.

Do not think you can keep up a habit of getting drunk and keep Christ at the same time. Christ is not going to share the throne of your heart with anything else.

## The Gospel as the Solution

Truly, if you want to experience the highs — to use that word — the solution is not drugs or substances. The solution is: be filled with the Spirit.

If the problem is sin, then the solution is the gospel. A pattern of drunkenness is not different from a pattern of sexual immorality or a pattern of theft or greed or selfish ambition. The resolution to all of these is Christ.

Someone might say this is a hateful message — that talking about drunkenness as sin marginalizes addicts and condemns them, making them feel worse about themselves. But here's the answer: they are already marginalized. We don't know what to do with the drunkard. We don't know what to do with the addict. They are already pushed to the margins of society, of the church, of families. Think about that one uncle that nobody talks about, who they try to keep outside if he comes to a party.

This view of drunkenness as sin is actually very freeing, because it tells us that we all struggle with desires that war within us. Even if you can't relate to drunkenness, you can relate to the idea of a war for your heart. And if sin is the problem, then the solution is the gospel — and we know what the gospel is.

Someone might say, "I'm concerned the drunkard will get stuck in guilt if we talk about sin." But do you think the drunkard doesn't feel guilty already? Do you think that when he sobers up he's not aware of the destruction he caused? Do you think the drug addict, when he comes down from his high, isn't already feeling guilty? Why do you think one of the motivations for the next hit, the next binge, is to blot out the memory of what he did during the last one?

The friend's job is to point the addict to the one who forgives, the one who liberates, the one who loves, and the one who empowers them to get out from under the dominion of sin and follow Jesus Christ.

When you talk about drunkenness as sin, sin should take us right to the cross. For every time we take a look at sin, we take ten looks at Jesus Christ.

## Is Drunkenness Really a Spiritual Issue?

Is it difficult to see the spiritual core of drunkenness? Ask yourself these questions:

- Is your struggle with drunkenness ever accompanied by the fear of the Lord? Are you ever thinking, "I'm going to get drunk today because my reverence for God is so great"?
- Do you have a keen sense of the presence and holiness of God when you struggle with the aftermath of getting drunk?
- Have you ever come out of a stupor and thought, "God was glorified in that experience — while my wife or husband was being destroyed, while my kids were being neglected, while I drove my friends away, God was glorified in that"?
- Have you ever had a sense of growing in repentance, faith, and obedience while indulging in drunkenness?

Addictions are incompatible with spiritual growth.

But let me finish with this, and it's true not just for those struggling with drunkenness but for anyone struggling with sin: do you feel hopeless at times? There's a lot of that in the church of Jesus Christ. If you think the knowledge of your sinfulness will intensify these problems, consider the example of the biblical writers. Facing their sins was hard and sometimes painful — but when confession of sin was linked to the knowledge of forgiveness of sin, hope and joy were the result.

In Psalm 130, the psalmist is in the deepest depths of misery — as low as you can get, his despair was such that he could feel death more than life. To bring him out of the pit, the Lord brought to his mind his great forgiveness, and the psalmist realized:

> *"If you, Lord, should mark iniquities, O Lord, who could stand? But there is forgiveness with you, that you may be feared."* (Psalm 130:3-4)

If you're struggling with drunkenness, there is forgiveness for you. If you're struggling with addiction, there is forgiveness for you. God does not want your perfection. God wants you to come to him knowing that you're a sinner, knowing that the Lord Jesus Christ lived his life for you, died the death you deserved, and was raised from the dead to declare that salvation is for sinners such as you.

Even as a believer struggling with a life-dominating sin, there is forgiveness in God. God doesn't love you any less because you're struggling. He loves you with the same love that sent his only begotten Son to the cross to die for you. And that's where the power to deliver you from the dominance of that sin in your life comes from.

Turn to Christ. Don't waste your drunkenness — to use a John Piper-ism: don't waste your drunkenness. Come to Christ. Believe in him. Receive his forgiveness. Be filled by his Spirit.
